action: Learn the five key consistency models in distributed systems. Test yourself with quiz mode.
description: Concept card: consistency models — strong, linearizable, sequential, causal, eventual.
context: A replica is a copy of your data on another machine. When you write data, it goes to one or more replicas. When you read, you read from a replica. The question consistency models answer: when you read from a replica, are you guaranteed to see the latest write? A 'read seeing a write' means the replica you're reading from has already received and applied that write. Different consistency models make different promises about this — stronger models guarantee you always see the latest write (but are slower), weaker models allow stale reads (but are faster). This is the most fundamental trade-off in distributed systems.
constraint_note: Focus on what each model guarantees and what it does NOT guarantee. The differences are subtle.
example: Linearizability is the strongest — operations appear instantaneous. Eventual is the weakest — replicas converge eventually but may disagree now.

items.reveal: Every operation appears to take effect atomically at some point between its invocation and response. All processes see the same order.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Real-time ordering — if op A completes before op B starts, everyone sees A before B

details.label: Cost
details.value: Requires coordination across nodes. High latency, especially cross-region

details.label: Used by
details.value: Spanner (TrueTime), etcd, ZooKeeper

items.reveal: All processes see operations in the same order, but that order doesn't have to match real-time. Each process's operations appear in program order.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Global total order exists, respects per-process ordering

details.label: Difference from linearizable
details.value: Two ops on different nodes may be reordered vs real time

details.label: Used by
details.value: Some memory models, ZooKeeper reads (within a session)

items.reveal: If operation A causally precedes B (A happened-before B), all nodes see A before B. Concurrent operations may be seen in different orders by different nodes.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Causally related operations are ordered; concurrent ones are not

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Can be implemented without global coordination — better availability

details.label: Used by
details.value: COPS, MongoDB (causal sessions)

items.reveal: A process always sees its own previous writes. Other processes may not see them yet.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: After writing X=5, your next read of X returns 5 (or later)

details.label: Common issue
details.value: User updates profile, refreshes page, sees old data — violates this

details.label: Implementation
details.value: Sticky sessions or read-from-primary after write

items.reveal: If no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. No ordering guarantees during convergence.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Convergence — given enough time and no new writes, all replicas agree

details.label: Risk
details.value: Stale reads, conflicting writes, no ordering between nodes

details.label: Used by
details.value: DynamoDB (default), Cassandra, DNS

action: Learn the three properties of the CAP theorem and what each actually means.
description: Concept card: CAP theorem — Consistency, Availability, Partition Tolerance.
context: CAP is the most cited (and most misunderstood) theorem in distributed systems. The key insight: during a network partition (which WILL happen), you must choose — either refuse requests to stay consistent (CP), or serve requests with potentially stale data to stay available (AP). You can't detect whether the other side is down or just unreachable, so you must pick a policy in advance.
constraint_note: Pay attention to the precise definitions — 'consistency' in CAP means linearizability specifically, not just 'data is correct'.
example: Imagine a network partition splits your system. Node A (in US) has the latest data. Node B (in EU) has stale data. A user in EU connects to Node B and requests their profile. You have two choices: (1) Refuse the request because you can't confirm it's up-to-date — this is choosing Consistency. (2) Return the potentially stale data so the user isn't blocked — this is choosing Availability. CAP says you MUST make this choice during a partition.

items.reveal: Every read receives the most recent write or an error. All nodes see the same data at the same time. This is specifically linearizability — not just 'no bugs'.

details.label: Precise meaning
details.value: If a write completes, all subsequent reads (on any node) return that value

details.label: Common confusion
details.value: CAP-consistency ≠ ACID-consistency. They mean different things

details.label: During partition
details.value: System returns errors rather than stale data

items.reveal: Every request to a non-failing node receives a response — no timeouts, no errors. The response doesn't have to be the most recent data.

details.label: Precise meaning
details.value: Every request eventually receives a non-error response, without guarantee of most recent write

details.label: Key nuance
details.value: A slow response still counts as available. An error does not.

details.label: During partition
details.value: System responds with potentially stale data rather than refusing

items.reveal: The system continues to operate despite arbitrary message loss or delay between nodes. Network partitions are a fact of life — you can't choose to not have P.

details.label: Precise meaning
details.value: System functions even when network messages between nodes are dropped or delayed indefinitely

details.label: Key insight
details.value: In any real distributed system, partitions WILL happen. P is not optional.

details.label: Implication
details.value: The real choice is between C and A during a partition — not 'pick 2 of 3'

action: Learn the three network timing models and which one matches real-world networks.
description: Concept card: network models — synchronous, asynchronous, partially synchronous.
context: The network model you assume determines what algorithms are possible and what guarantees you can provide. In a synchronous model, you can reliably detect failures using timeouts — if no response in Δ time, the node is dead. In an asynchronous model, you can't — a slow node looks identical to a dead one. The FLP impossibility result (no deterministic consensus in async systems) only applies to the asynchronous model. Raft and Paxos work because they assume partial synchrony — the network is usually well-behaved, and timeouts work most of the time.
constraint_note: Real networks are partially synchronous — they usually behave well but can have unbounded delays during failures.
example: Raft assumes a partially synchronous model — it uses timeouts to detect failures, which wouldn't work in a purely asynchronous model.

items.reveal: Messages are delivered within a known, bounded time. Process execution speeds have known bounds. You can reliably detect failures via timeouts.

details.label: Assumption
details.value: There exists a known upper bound Δ on message delivery time

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Failure detection is reliable — if no response in Δ, node is down

details.label: Reality
details.value: Too optimistic for real networks. GC pauses, network congestion break the assumption

items.reveal: No assumptions about timing. Messages can take arbitrarily long. No way to distinguish a slow node from a dead one.

details.label: Assumption
details.value: Messages eventually arrive but with no upper bound on delay

details.label: Consequence
details.value: Cannot use timeouts for failure detection. FLP impossibility applies.

details.label: Reality
details.value: Too pessimistic — real networks usually deliver messages quickly

items.reveal: The system behaves synchronously most of the time, but can have periods of asynchrony (unbounded delays). After some unknown time, synchrony returns.

details.label: Assumption
details.value: There exists a bound Δ, but it's unknown, or only holds after some unknown time T

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Matches real networks. Allows practical algorithms like Raft that use timeouts

details.label: Used by
details.value: Raft, Paxos (in practice), most production consensus protocols

action: Learn the four types of process failures in distributed systems.
description: Concept card: failure models — crash-stop, crash-recovery, omission, Byzantine.
context: The failure model determines how hard your problem is. Crash failures are easy to reason about. Byzantine failures are the hardest — and most expensive to tolerate.
constraint_note: Each model is strictly harder than the previous. Algorithms designed for Byzantine failures also handle crash failures, but not vice versa.
example: Most production systems assume crash-recovery (nodes can crash and restart). Only systems handling untrusted participants (blockchains) need Byzantine tolerance.

items.reveal: A node stops executing and never recovers. Once it crashes, it's gone forever. Other nodes can eventually detect its absence.

details.label: Detection
details.value: Eventually detectable via timeouts (in partially synchronous model)

details.label: Tolerance
details.value: Need 2f+1 nodes to tolerate f crash failures for consensus

details.label: Example
details.value: Hardware failure with no restart, decommissioned server

items.reveal: A node can crash and later restart, potentially with some state recovered from stable storage (disk). May miss messages sent during downtime.

details.label: Key challenge
details.value: Node must know what it promised before crashing — needs write-ahead log

details.label: Tolerance
details.value: Algorithms must handle nodes that forget in-memory state but retain disk state

details.label: Example
details.value: Server reboot, process restart, JVM crash with disk intact

items.reveal: A node is running but fails to send or receive some messages. It doesn't crash — it just silently drops messages.

details.label: Send omission
details.value: Node processes request but response never leaves

details.label: Receive omission
details.value: Message arrives at network layer but node never processes it

details.label: Example
details.value: Full message queue, network buffer overflow, firewall rule

items.reveal: A node can behave arbitrarily — send wrong data, lie, collude with other faulty nodes, or act maliciously. The most general and hardest failure model.

details.label: Tolerance
details.value: Need 3f+1 nodes to tolerate f Byzantine failures

details.label: Cost
details.value: Much more expensive than crash tolerance — more messages, more nodes

details.label: Example
details.value: Compromised server, software bug causing wrong responses, blockchain validators

action: Learn the three RPC delivery semantics and when to use each.
description: Concept card: RPC semantics — at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once.
context: An RPC (Remote Procedure Call) is calling a function on another machine over the network. Your code calls paymentService.charge(userId, amount) and it looks like a local function call, but under the hood it serializes the arguments, sends them over the network, the remote machine executes the function, and sends back the result. The problem: three things can go wrong. (1) Your request never arrives — the network dropped it. (2) The remote machine processes it but the response is lost — you don't know it succeeded. (3) The remote machine crashes mid-processing — it may or may not have completed. In all three cases, you see the same thing: a timeout. You can't tell which happened. This ambiguity is why RPC semantics matter.
constraint_note: Exactly-once is what everyone wants but is impossible to achieve in the general case. Real systems approximate it with idempotency + at-least-once.
example: A payment API should be idempotent (same request ID → same result) so that retries don't double-charge. That's at-least-once delivery with idempotent handling.

items.reveal: The client sends the request once and never retries. The operation executes zero or one times. Simple but may lose requests.

details.label: Implementation
details.value: Send request, start timeout, if no response — report failure

details.label: Risk
details.value: Lost requests are never retried. Operation may not happen.

details.label: Good for
details.value: Non-critical reads, analytics events, logging

items.reveal: The client retries until it gets a response. The operation executes one or more times. Requires the operation to be idempotent to be safe.

details.label: Implementation
details.value: Send request, if timeout → retry. Keep retrying with backoff.

details.label: Risk
details.value: Duplicate execution if server processed request but response was lost

details.label: Good for
details.value: Idempotent operations — reads, upserts, operations with unique request IDs

items.reveal: The operation executes exactly one time, regardless of failures. Impossible in the general case over unreliable networks. Approximated via idempotency keys + deduplication.

details.label: Why impossible
details.value: If server crashes after executing but before responding, client can't know — Two Generals Problem

details.label: Approximation
details.value: At-least-once delivery + server-side deduplication using unique request IDs

details.label: Used by
details.value: Kafka (idempotent producer), payment systems (idempotency keys)

action: Learn the three levels of ordering guarantees in distributed systems.
description: Concept card: ordering guarantees — FIFO, causal, total ordering.
context: Ordering is one of the hardest problems in distributed systems. In a single-threaded program, everything happens in order. In a distributed system, 'order' is ambiguous — different nodes may see events in different sequences.
constraint_note: Each level is strictly stronger (and more expensive) than the previous. Total ordering requires global coordination.
example: In a chat app: User A asks 'What time is the meeting?' User B replies 'It's at 3pm.' Without ordering guarantees, a third user might see 'It's at 3pm' appear BEFORE 'What time is the meeting?' — the answer before the question. FIFO ordering prevents this within one sender. Causal ordering prevents it across senders (the reply is causally related to the question). Total ordering ensures everyone sees all messages in the same sequence.

items.reveal: Messages from the same sender are delivered in the order they were sent. No guarantees about ordering between different senders.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: If process P sends m1 then m2, every recipient sees m1 before m2

details.label: Cost
details.value: Low — just sequence numbers per sender

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Two messages from different senders may be seen in different orders by different nodes

items.reveal: If message m1 causally precedes m2 (m1 'happened before' m2), then all nodes deliver m1 before m2. Concurrent messages may be delivered in any order.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Causally related messages are ordered; concurrent messages are not

details.label: Cost
details.value: Moderate — requires vector clocks or similar mechanism to track causality

details.label: Example
details.value: In a chat: a reply is always seen after the message it replies to

items.reveal: All nodes deliver all messages in exactly the same order. If any node sees m1 before m2, every node sees m1 before m2.

details.label: Guarantee
details.value: Single global order that all nodes agree on

details.label: Cost
details.value: High — requires a leader or consensus protocol (Raft, Paxos)

details.label: Used by
details.value: Replicated state machines, database replication logs, Raft log

action: Fill in the blanks to test your understanding of the CAP theorem and PACELC.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: CAP theorem and PACELC extension.
context: CAP is the most-cited theorem in distributed systems. PACELC extends it to cover the common case when there's no partition. Precise recall matters here.
constraint_note: Try to answer from memory before revealing. These are foundational — you should be able to state them precisely.

items.prompt: The CAP theorem states that during a network ___, a distributed system must choose between ___ and ___.
items.hint: The three letters of CAP

items.prompt: In CAP, 'consistency' specifically means ___ — the strongest consistency model.
items.hint: Every read returns the most recent write

items.prompt: Partition tolerance is not optional because network ___ will happen in any real distributed system.
items.hint: Messages lost or delayed between nodes

items.prompt: PACELC extends CAP: even when there is no partition (E), you must choose between ___ and ___.
items.hint: The L and C in PACELC

items.prompt: A system that chooses availability during partitions and low latency otherwise is classified as PA/___.
items.hint: Else Latency — like DynamoDB and Cassandra

action: Fill in the blanks about time, ordering, and clocks in distributed systems.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: Lamport timestamps, vector clocks, happens-before.
context: There's no global clock in a distributed system. Logical clocks are the fundamental tool for reasoning about event ordering without synchronized physical clocks.
constraint_note: These are precise definitions. Close paraphrases count, but try to be exact.

items.prompt: The happens-before relation was defined by ___ in his 1978 paper 'Time, Clocks, and the Ordering of Events'.
items.hint: Also known for Paxos and LaTeX

items.prompt: Lamport timestamps provide a ___ order of events, but cannot detect ___ events.
items.hint: They order everything, even things that don't need ordering

items.prompt: Vector clocks use a vector of ___ counters, one per ___ in the system.
items.hint: Each process tracks its own counter and what it knows about others

items.prompt: Two events a and b are concurrent if neither ___ the other in the happens-before relation.
items.hint: a ↛ b and b ↛ a

action: Fill in the blanks about consistency models.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: consistency model definitions and properties.
context: Consistency models are the contract between a distributed system and its users. Getting the definitions wrong leads to building systems that don't provide the guarantees you think they do.
constraint_note: Precision matters. 'Eventually consistent' and 'strongly consistent' are not enough — know the exact guarantees.

items.prompt: Linearizability requires that every operation appears to take effect ___ at some point between its invocation and ___.
items.hint: It looks like a single-copy system

items.prompt: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new ___ are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same ___.
items.hint: Given enough time and silence...

items.prompt: Causal consistency ensures that operations related by the ___ relation are seen in the same order by all nodes.
items.hint: If A caused B, everyone sees A before B

action: Fill in the blanks about the FLP impossibility result and fundamental limits.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: FLP impossibility, Two Generals, fundamental limits.
context: Knowing what's impossible is as important as knowing what's possible. These impossibility results shape every practical distributed system design.
constraint_note: These are precise theoretical results. The exact conditions matter.

items.prompt: The FLP impossibility result states that no ___ consensus protocol can guarantee termination in an ___ system with even one faulty process.
items.hint: Named after Fischer, Lynch, and Paterson (1985)

items.prompt: FLP implies that practical consensus protocols like Raft use ___ to detect failures, which only works in a ___ synchronous model.
items.hint: Raft's election timeout is the key mechanism

items.prompt: The Two Generals Problem shows that ___ agreement is impossible over an unreliable communication channel.
items.hint: Neither general can be sure the other received the message

items.prompt: A distributed system is one in which the failure of a computer you didn't even know existed can render your own computer ___. — Leslie Lamport
items.hint: The most famous definition of a distributed system

action: Fill in the blanks about network models and RPC.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: network models, RPC semantics, basic properties.
context: The network model you assume determines what's achievable. RPC semantics determine how your system handles the fundamental ambiguity of network calls.
constraint_note: Think about what each model assumes and what breaks when those assumptions are violated.

items.prompt: In a synchronous network model, there is a known upper bound on message ___ time.
items.hint: Messages arrive within Δ time units

items.prompt: The asynchronous model makes ___ assumptions about timing, making it the most ___ but hardest model.
items.hint: No upper bound on anything — fewest assumptions

items.prompt: At-least-once RPC delivery requires the operation to be ___ to be safe, because the same request may execute multiple times.
items.hint: Same input → same result, no matter how many times

items.prompt: True exactly-once delivery is ___ in the general case. Real systems approximate it with at-least-once plus ___.
items.hint: Two Generals Problem prevents certainty

action: Compare strong consistency vs eventual consistency. Understand the trade-off.
description: Trade-off comparison: strong consistency vs eventual consistency.
context: This is the most fundamental trade-off in distributed systems. Every system sits somewhere on this spectrum, and the choice affects latency, availability, and correctness.
constraint_note: Neither is 'better' — they serve different needs. The question is always: what does your application require?
example: A bank balance needs strong consistency (no stale reads). A social media like count can tolerate eventual consistency (off by a few for a second is fine).

pairs.left: Strong Consistency — every read returns the most recent write. Requires coordination across nodes. Higher latency, lower availability during partitions. Used by: Spanner, CockroachDB, etcd.
pairs.right: Eventual Consistency — reads may return stale data temporarily. No coordination needed for reads. Lower latency, higher availability. Used by: DynamoDB (default), Cassandra, DNS.

action: Compare synchronous vs asynchronous replication. Understand what you gain and lose with each.
description: Trade-off comparison: synchronous vs asynchronous replication.
context: How you replicate data determines your durability and latency guarantees. This choice is made in every replicated database, message queue, and distributed storage system.
constraint_note: Think about what happens when a replica is slow or unreachable in each model.
example: PostgreSQL streaming replication can be sync (safe but slow) or async (fast but may lose recent writes on failover).

pairs.left: Synchronous Replication — write confirmed only after all (or a quorum of) replicas acknowledge. Guarantees no data loss on failover. Risk: one slow replica blocks all writes. Higher write latency.
pairs.right: Asynchronous Replication — write confirmed after primary writes locally. Replicas updated in background. Lower write latency. Risk: data loss if primary fails before replication completes.

action: Compare CP systems vs AP systems. Know real examples of each.
description: Trade-off comparison: CP vs AP systems during network partitions.
context: During a network partition, every distributed system must make a choice. Understanding which choice a system makes — and why — is core to distributed systems reasoning.
constraint_note: Remember: this choice only matters DURING a partition. When the network is healthy, you can have both C and A.
example: ZooKeeper is CP — it refuses writes if it can't reach a quorum. Cassandra is AP — it accepts writes on any reachable node and reconciles later.

pairs.left: CP (Consistency + Partition tolerance) — during a partition, refuses to serve requests rather than return stale/inconsistent data. May become unavailable. Examples: ZooKeeper, etcd, HBase, Spanner.
pairs.right: AP (Availability + Partition tolerance) — during a partition, continues serving requests on reachable nodes. May return stale data. Reconciles when partition heals. Examples: Cassandra, CouchDB, DynamoDB (default).

action: Compare physical clocks vs logical clocks. Understand why distributed systems need logical clocks.
description: Trade-off comparison: physical (wall) clocks vs logical clocks for ordering events.
context: You can't trust wall clocks in a distributed system — they drift, they skew, NTP corrections can jump backward. Logical clocks solve ordering without relying on synchronized time.
constraint_note: Physical clocks tell you 'when' (approximately). Logical clocks tell you 'what happened before what' (exactly, for causally related events).
example: Google Spanner is the rare exception — it uses GPS + atomic clocks (TrueTime) to bound clock uncertainty, enabling physical-time-based ordering. Most systems can't do this.

pairs.left: Physical Clocks — wall-clock time from NTP. Can drift between nodes (milliseconds to seconds). NTP corrections can cause time to jump backward. Last-write-wins with physical timestamps can lose data. Exception: Spanner's TrueTime.
pairs.right: Logical Clocks — counters incremented on events and message exchanges. Lamport timestamps: total order but can't detect concurrency. Vector clocks: detect concurrency but grow with number of nodes. No dependency on synchronized time.

action: Read the scenario and write your analysis: what happens, what goes wrong, and how would you handle it?
description: What-breaks scenario: network partition between primary and replica.
context: Network partitions are the defining challenge of distributed systems. This scenario tests whether you can reason through the consequences of a partition on a real system.
constraint_note: Consider both sides of the partition. What do users on each side experience? What happens when the partition heals?
example: Think about: Can users still read? Can they write? What happens to writes made on both sides during the partition?

reference.body:
You have a primary database in US-East and a read replica in EU-West with asynchronous replication. A network partition occurs between the two regions — they can't communicate. Users in both regions are still sending requests.

Analyze:
1. What happens to read requests in EU-West?
2. What happens to write requests in EU-West?
3. What happens when the partition heals?
4. If you switched to synchronous replication, how would the behavior change during the partition?

action: Read the scenario and write your analysis of what goes wrong with clock-based ordering.
description: What-breaks scenario: clock skew with last-write-wins.
context: Using wall-clock timestamps for conflict resolution is one of the most common mistakes in distributed systems. This scenario shows exactly how it fails.
constraint_note: Think about what 'last' means when clocks disagree. Which write actually happened last in real time?
example: Consider: if Server A's clock is ahead, its writes always 'win' even if they happened earlier in real time.

reference.body:
Your distributed key-value store uses last-write-wins (LWW) with wall-clock timestamps for conflict resolution. Server A's clock is 5 seconds ahead of Server B's clock due to NTP drift.

At real-world time T=100:
- Server A receives: SET user:123 name='Alice' (timestamp: 105 due to clock skew)
- Server B receives: SET user:123 name='Bob' (timestamp: 100, accurate clock)

Analyze:
1. Which write 'wins' under LWW? Is that correct?
2. What if the clocks were skewed the other way?
3. How could you solve this without fixing clock sync?
4. What does Cassandra do about this problem?

action: Read the scenario and analyze the RPC ambiguity. What are your options?
description: What-breaks scenario: RPC timeout on a payment request.
context: The fundamental ambiguity of RPCs: when a call times out, you don't know if it succeeded or failed. This is the Two Generals Problem in practice, and it affects every networked system.
constraint_note: There are exactly three possibilities when an RPC times out. Consider each one and what retrying does in each case.
example: The three possibilities: (1) request never arrived, (2) server processed it but response was lost, (3) server is still processing it.

reference.body:
Your e-commerce service calls a payment API to charge a customer $50. The request times out after 5 seconds. You don't know if the payment was processed.

Analyze:
1. What are the three possible states of the payment?
2. If you retry the request, what's the risk?
3. If you DON'T retry, what's the risk?
4. How would you design the payment API to make retries safe?
5. What's the role of an idempotency key here?

action: Walk through how Lamport timestamps work step by step. Follow the trace carefully.
description: Protocol walkthrough: Lamport timestamps — step-by-step trace with 3 processes.
context: Lamport timestamps are the simplest logical clock. Understanding the algorithm by tracing through an example builds intuition for how logical time works.
constraint_note: Rule: on local event, increment counter. On send, increment and attach. On receive, set counter to max(local, received) + 1.
example: After this trace, you should be able to assign Lamport timestamps to any set of events.

steps: Three processes start with Lamport clock = 0: P1(0), P2(0), P3(0)
P1 does a local event → P1 increments: P1(1)
P1 sends message m1 to P2 with timestamp 1 → P1(1)
P2 receives m1: max(P2=0, received=1) + 1 = 2 → P2(2)
P2 does a local event → P2(3)
P2 sends message m2 to P3 with timestamp 3 → P2(3)
P3 does a local event before receiving m2 → P3(1)
P3 receives m2: max(P3=1, received=3) + 1 = 4 → P3(4)
Result: m1 has timestamp 1, m2 has timestamp 3. Lamport ordering: m1 < m2. This matches causality — m1 caused m2 (P2 received m1 before sending m2).
Key limitation: P3's local event (timestamp 1) appears 'before' m2 (timestamp 3) in Lamport order, but they might be concurrent. Lamport timestamps can't tell.

action: Walk through how vector clocks detect concurrent events. Follow the trace and compare with Lamport timestamps.
description: Protocol walkthrough: vector clocks — detecting concurrency between events.
context: Vector clocks solve the key limitation of Lamport timestamps: they can distinguish 'happened-before' from 'concurrent'. This is essential for conflict detection in replicated systems.
constraint_note: Rule: on local event, increment own entry. On send, increment own entry and attach full vector. On receive, take element-wise max, then increment own entry.
example: Two events are concurrent if neither vector clock dominates the other (some entries are greater, some are smaller).

steps: Two processes start: P1=[0,0], P2=[0,0]
P1 does a local write (set X=1): P1 increments own entry → P1=[1,0]
P2 does a local write (set X=2): P2 increments own entry → P2=[0,1]
Are these concurrent? Compare [1,0] vs [0,1]: P1 has higher first entry, P2 has higher second entry. Neither dominates → CONCURRENT. Conflict detected!
Now P1 sends its state to P2. P2 receives: max([0,1],[1,0]) = [1,1], then increments own → P2=[1,2]
P2 resolves the conflict (e.g., picks X=2) and now has vector [1,2]
P2 does another write: P2=[1,3]
Compare P1=[1,0] with P2=[1,3]: P2 dominates (1≥1 and 3≥0) → P2's write happened AFTER P1's. Not concurrent.
Key insight: vector clocks grow with the number of processes. With N processes, each clock is a vector of N integers. This limits scalability.

action: A guided thinking exercise: analyze a system you use or build through the lens of distributed systems fundamentals.
description: Guided thinking: where's the state in a system you know?
context: The best way to internalize distributed systems concepts is to apply them to systems you already know. This exercise connects theory to your daily experience.
constraint_note: Think of a real system — something you use at work or a product you use daily. Be specific.
example: Works for: your team's backend, Gmail, Uber, your company's database, a messaging app.

segments.text: Think of a system you use or work on every day. Something real. Hold it in your mind.
Where is the state stored? Is it in one place or many? Is it replicated?
What consistency model does it use? Would you notice if a read returned slightly stale data?
What happens if the primary data store goes down right now? How long until someone notices? How long until it's fixed?
Is this system CP or AP? During a network issue, does it refuse requests or serve potentially stale data?
What's the single point of failure? Every system has one — or has explicitly designed around it.
If you could change one thing about this system's distributed design, what would it be?

action: A guided thinking exercise: reason through what happens when a network call fails mid-request.
description: Guided thinking: the ambiguity of network failures.
context: Network failures are the defining experience of distributed systems. This exercise builds the reflex of thinking through failure cases — a skill that separates senior from junior engineers.
constraint_note: Think about a real API call you've made or a system you've built. Be concrete.
example: Works for: any API call, database write, payment processing, message sending.

segments.text: Think of the last time you made an API call in code you wrote. What was it? A database write? A third-party API?
Now imagine that call times out. What are the possible states? Did it succeed? Fail? Is it still running?
If you retry, what happens in each case? What if the original request actually succeeded?
Is the operation idempotent? If you send it twice, do you get the same result or a duplicate?
How would you make it safe to retry? Think about idempotency keys, unique request IDs, or conditional writes.
Here's the uncomfortable truth: in a distributed system, you can never be 100% sure a remote operation succeeded. You can only design for safe retries.

action: Practice a system design interview dialogue about CAP theorem trade-offs.
description: Interview dialogue: discussing CAP theorem trade-offs for a user profile service.
context: In system design interviews, you're expected to reason about consistency trade-offs out loud. This dialogue practices that skill with a realistic scenario.
constraint_note: Read the interviewer's questions and think about your answer before revealing the candidate's response.
example: The goal is to practice articulating trade-offs clearly, not to memorize answers.

lines.speaker: Interviewer
lines.text: You're designing a user profile service used globally. Users can update their display name. How do you handle consistency?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: First question: how bad is it if a user updates their name and someone else sees the old name for a few seconds?

lines.speaker: Interviewer
lines.text: Not great, but not catastrophic. What about the user themselves seeing the old name after updating?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: That's worse — it violates read-your-writes consistency. The user thinks the update failed. I'd ensure read-your-writes for the updating user, and eventual consistency for everyone else.

lines.speaker: Interviewer
lines.text: How would you implement that?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: After a write, route that user's reads to the primary for a short window — say 5 seconds. Everyone else reads from replicas. This gives the updater strong consistency without paying the latency cost for all reads.

lines.speaker: Interviewer
lines.text: What happens during a network partition between US and EU?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: For profile reads, I'd choose availability — serve potentially stale data from the local replica. For profile writes, I'd accept the write locally and replicate when the partition heals. Conflict resolution via last-write-wins is fine for display names.

lines.speaker: Interviewer
lines.text: So this is an AP system?

lines.speaker: You
lines.text: For profiles, yes. But I'd make this a per-feature decision. Profile names can be AP. But if this service also handled account deletion or email changes, those should be CP — refuse the operation if we can't confirm it globally.

action: Listen to (or read) the story of the Two Generals Problem, then answer the comprehension questions.
description: Narration: the Two Generals Problem — why perfect agreement is impossible over unreliable channels.
context: The Two Generals Problem is the oldest impossibility result in distributed computing. It explains why every networked system must deal with uncertainty — and why exactly-once delivery is a myth.
constraint_note: This is a thought experiment, not a real military scenario. Focus on why no finite number of messages can solve the problem.
example: After this, you should understand why an RPC timeout leaves you in an uncertain state — it's the Two Generals Problem in disguise.

segments.text: Two armies are camped on opposite sides of a valley. They need to attack a city at the same time to win. If only one attacks, it loses.
They can only communicate by sending messengers through the valley. But messengers can be captured — delivery is not guaranteed.
General A sends a message: 'Attack at dawn.' But A doesn't know if the messenger arrived. So A waits for confirmation.
General B receives the message and sends back: 'Confirmed, attack at dawn.' But now B doesn't know if the confirmation arrived.
So B waits for a confirmation of the confirmation. And A would need to confirm that confirmation. This goes on forever.
No finite number of messages can give both generals certainty. There's always a last message that might have been lost.
This is exactly what happens with network calls. When your request times out, you're General A — you don't know if the other side received your message and acted on it.

questions.prompt: Why can't the generals just send enough confirmations to be sure?
questions.answer: There's always a last message that might be lost. No finite chain of confirmations provides certainty.

questions.prompt: How does this relate to RPC timeouts?
questions.answer: When an RPC times out, you don't know if the server received and processed the request — same uncertainty as the generals.

questions.prompt: What does this imply about exactly-once delivery?
questions.answer: True exactly-once delivery is impossible over unreliable networks. You can only approximate it with idempotency.

action: Review the key mental models for distributed systems thinking. These are the reflexes that experts use.
description: Concept card: the five mental models of distributed systems thinking.
context: Distributed systems mastery isn't about memorizing algorithms — it's about developing thinking reflexes. These five questions are what experienced engineers ask automatically when looking at any system.
constraint_note: These aren't facts to memorize — they're habits to build. Practice asking these questions about every system you encounter.
example: Next time you look at an architecture diagram, run through all five questions. You'll spot issues that others miss.

items.reveal: Identify every place data is stored — databases, caches, queues, local memory, browser storage. State is where bugs hide and where failures hurt.

details.label: Follow-up
details.value: Is the state replicated? How many copies? Are they consistent?

items.reveal: For every component, ask: what if it crashes? What if it's slow? What if the network between here and there breaks? Design for failure, not for success.

details.label: Follow-up
details.value: How long until someone notices? How long until it's fixed? What's the blast radius?

items.reveal: Every design choice has a cost. Choosing strong consistency means higher latency. Choosing availability means stale reads. Name the trade-off explicitly.

details.label: Follow-up
details.value: Is this trade-off acceptable for our use case? Who decided?

items.reveal: If traffic grows 10x, what breaks first? The database? The network? A single-threaded component? Think in powers of 10, not percentages.

details.label: Follow-up
details.value: What's the bottleneck? Is it compute, storage, network, or coordination?

items.primary: How do we know it's working?
items.reveal: If this system is silently returning wrong data, how would you detect it? What metrics, logs, or alerts would tell you something is wrong?

details.label: Follow-up
details.value: What's the SLO? What's the error budget? Who gets paged?

action: Learn the three replication strategies and when to use each.
description: Concept card: single-leader, multi-leader, and leaderless replication.
context: These three strategies form a progression. Single-leader is simplest: one node handles all writes, no conflicts possible. But the leader is a bottleneck and single point of failure. Multi-leader removes the single-writer bottleneck by allowing writes in multiple locations, but now you have conflicts to resolve. Leaderless goes further — no designated leader at all, any node accepts writes — but requires quorum math and conflict resolution. Each strategy solves a problem the previous one couldn't.
constraint_note: Focus on the trade-offs, not just the definitions. Each strategy makes a different bet about what matters most.
example: PostgreSQL uses single-leader. CouchDB supports multi-leader. Cassandra and DynamoDB use leaderless.

items.reveal: One node (leader/primary) handles all writes. Followers replicate the leader's write log and serve reads. Simple, well-understood, but the leader is a bottleneck and single point of failure.

details.label: Write path
details.value: Client → leader → replicate to followers (sync or async)

details.label: Advantage
details.value: No write conflicts — all writes go through one node. Easy to reason about.

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Leader is a write bottleneck. Failover can lose data (async) or block writes (sync).

details.label: Used by
details.value: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB (replica sets), Redis Sentinel

items.reveal: Multiple nodes accept writes independently. Each leader replicates to the others. Enables writes in multiple datacenters but introduces write conflicts that must be resolved.

details.label: Write path
details.value: Client → any leader → asynchronously replicate to other leaders

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Write availability in multiple regions. Tolerates datacenter outages.

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Write conflicts are inevitable. Conflict resolution is complex and error-prone.

details.label: Used by
details.value: CouchDB, Tungsten Replicator for MySQL, collaborative editing (Google Docs)

items.reveal: Any node can accept reads and writes. Client sends writes to multiple nodes in parallel. Reads query multiple nodes and use version numbers to determine the latest value.

details.label: Write path
details.value: Client → send to W nodes in parallel → success when W acknowledge

details.label: Advantage
details.value: No single point of failure. No failover needed. High availability.

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Quorum math required. Stale reads possible. Conflict resolution needed for concurrent writes.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Cassandra, DynamoDB, Riak, Voldemort

action: Learn the three problems caused by replication lag. These are the most common real-world replication bugs.
description: Concept card: replication lag problems — read-your-writes, monotonic reads, consistent prefix reads.
context: Replication lag is the time delay between when a write is applied to the leader and when it arrives at a follower. If the leader accepts a write at 14:00:00.000 and the follower receives it at 14:00:00.500, the replication lag is 500ms. During those 500ms, anyone reading from the follower sees OLD data — the write hasn't arrived yet. This lag is usually small (milliseconds) but can spike during high load, network issues, or follower recovery. The three problems below are the specific bugs that replication lag causes.
constraint_note: Each problem has a specific name and a specific fix. Don't confuse them — they're different failure modes.
example: User updates their profile, refreshes the page, and sees the OLD profile. That's a read-your-writes violation — the read hit a replica that hadn't caught up yet.

items.reveal: After a user writes data, they should always see their own write on subsequent reads. Violated when a write goes to the leader but the next read hits a lagging follower.

details.label: Symptom
details.value: User updates profile → refreshes → sees old data → thinks update failed → submits again

details.label: Fix
details.value: Read from leader for data the user recently modified. Or track write timestamp and only read from replicas caught up to that point.

details.label: Also called
details.value: Read-after-write consistency

items.reveal: If a user reads a value, subsequent reads should not return an older value. Violated when sequential reads hit different replicas at different lag levels.

details.label: Symptom
details.value: User sees a comment, refreshes, comment disappears, refreshes again, it's back. Time travel.

details.label: Fix
details.value: Sticky sessions — route each user's reads to the same replica consistently.

details.label: Key insight
details.value: Stronger than eventual consistency but weaker than strong consistency

items.reveal: If a sequence of writes happens in a certain order, anyone reading those writes should see them in the same order. Violated when causally related writes are replicated at different speeds.

details.label: Symptom
details.value: In a chat: answer appears before the question it's replying to, because the answer's partition replicated faster

details.label: Fix
details.value: Ensure causally related writes go to the same partition, or use causal ordering mechanisms

details.label: Tricky because
details.value: Requires tracking causal dependencies between writes across partitions

action: Learn the four strategies for resolving write conflicts in replicated systems.
description: Concept card: conflict resolution — LWW, merge functions, CRDTs, application-level resolution.
context: When two nodes accept conflicting writes (multi-leader or leaderless), the system must decide which value wins. There's no universally correct answer — each strategy trades off simplicity, correctness, and data loss.
constraint_note: LWW is the simplest but silently loses data. CRDTs are the most elegant but only work for specific data types. Know the trade-offs.
example: Two users edit the same profile simultaneously on different nodes. Node A receives: SET name='Alice'. Node B receives: SET name='Bob'. Both writes succeed locally. When the nodes sync, they discover two different values for the same key — this is a write conflict. Which name should the profile show? Alice? Bob? Both? The four strategies below are different answers to this question, each with different trade-offs.

items.reveal: Attach a timestamp to each write. The write with the highest timestamp wins. All other concurrent writes are silently discarded.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Simple to implement. Guaranteed convergence. No merge logic needed.

details.label: Problem
details.value: Silently drops data. If two users write concurrently, one write is lost with no notification.

details.label: Clock issue
details.value: Relies on timestamps — clock skew can cause the 'wrong' write to win

details.label: Used by
details.value: Cassandra (default), DynamoDB (optionally)

items.reveal: Application provides a function that takes conflicting values and produces a merged result. Can preserve all information but requires domain-specific logic.

details.label: Example
details.value: Shopping cart: merge by taking the union of items from both versions

details.label: Advantage
details.value: No data loss if merge function is well-designed

details.label: Problem
details.value: Merge functions are hard to write correctly. Must be commutative and associative.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Riak (allow_mult), CouchDB (conflict revisions)

items.reveal: Conflict-free Replicated Data Types — data structures designed so that concurrent updates can always be merged automatically without conflicts. Convergence is mathematically guaranteed.

details.label: How
details.value: Operations are commutative and associative — order doesn't matter, result is the same

details.label: Examples
details.value: G-Counter (grow-only), PN-Counter (add/subtract), OR-Set (add/remove), LWW-Register

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Only works for specific data types. Can't CRDT-ify arbitrary application logic.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Redis (CRDT-based), Riak, Automerge, Yjs (collaborative editing)

items.reveal: Store all conflicting versions (siblings). Present them to the application or user to resolve manually. Most correct but adds complexity to the application.

details.label: How
details.value: Database stores all conflicting versions. Application reads them all and decides.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: No data loss. Domain-appropriate resolution.

details.label: Problem
details.value: Application must handle conflicts — adds code complexity. Users may see confusing prompts.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Git (merge conflicts), CouchDB (conflict revisions), Google Docs (operational transform)

action: Learn common quorum configurations and what each optimizes for.
description: Concept card: quorum configurations — how R, W, and N values affect consistency and performance.
context: A quorum is the minimum number of nodes that must participate in an operation for it to be valid. In quorum replication, N is the total number of replicas, W is how many must acknowledge a write, and R is how many must respond to a read. HOW a quorum write works: the client sends the write to all N replicas. It waits for W of them to confirm. Once W confirm, the write is considered successful. HOW a quorum read works: the client asks R replicas for the value. It compares their responses (using version numbers) and returns the newest. WHY R + W > N guarantees consistency: if you wrote to W nodes and read from R nodes, and W + R > N, then at least one node is in BOTH sets — it has the latest write and will be included in the read.
constraint_note: R + W > N guarantees overlap between read and write sets. If R + W ≤ N, stale reads are possible.
example: With N=3, W=2, R=2: every read contacts at least one node that has the latest write. This is the most common configuration.

items.reveal: R+W=4 > 3. Reads and writes both tolerate 1 node failure. Good balance of read/write performance and consistency.

details.label: Write tolerance
details.value: 1 node can be down and writes still succeed

details.label: Read tolerance
details.value: 1 node can be down and reads still succeed

details.label: Consistency
details.value: Guaranteed — read set always overlaps with write set

items.reveal: R+W=4 > 3. Writes are fast (only 1 ack needed) but reads must contact all nodes. Good for write-heavy workloads.

details.label: Write tolerance
details.value: 2 nodes can be down and writes still succeed

details.label: Read tolerance
details.value: 0 — all nodes must be up for reads

details.label: Trade-off
details.value: Very fast writes, but reads are slow and fragile

items.reveal: R+W=4 > 3. Reads are fast (only 1 node needed) but writes require all nodes. Good for read-heavy workloads.

details.label: Write tolerance
details.value: 0 — all nodes must be up for writes

details.label: Read tolerance
details.value: 2 nodes can be down and reads still succeed

details.label: Trade-off
details.value: Very fast reads, but writes are slow and fragile

items.reveal: R+W=3 = N. NOT greater than N. Read set may not overlap with write set. Stale reads are possible. This is eventual consistency, not strong.

details.label: Why it fails
details.value: With W=2, the write goes to 2 of 3 nodes. With R=1, the read hits 1 node — which might be the one that didn't get the write.

details.label: When used
details.value: When you want fast reads and can tolerate stale data

details.label: Key lesson
details.value: R + W must be STRICTLY GREATER than N for consistency

action: Learn the four types of replication logs and the trade-offs of each.
description: Concept card: replication log types — statement-based, WAL, logical (row-based), trigger-based.
context: The replication log is HOW changes propagate from leader to followers. The choice affects compatibility, performance, and what kinds of operations can be replicated correctly.
constraint_note: Statement-based replication seems simplest but has subtle bugs. Logical replication is the most flexible and is now the standard approach.
example: PostgreSQL uses WAL shipping for physical replication and logical decoding for logical replication. MySQL uses row-based binary log by default.

items.reveal: Leader logs every write statement (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) and sends it to followers, which execute the same statements.

details.label: Problem
details.value: Non-deterministic functions break it: NOW(), RAND(), auto-increment produce different values on different nodes

details.label: Problem
details.value: Statements with side effects (triggers, stored procedures) may produce different results

details.label: Status
details.value: Largely abandoned. MySQL switched to row-based by default.

items.reveal: Leader ships its write-ahead log — the exact byte-level changes to disk blocks. Followers apply the same byte changes to their storage.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Exact physical copy. Simple and reliable.

details.label: Problem
details.value: Tied to storage engine internals. Leader and follower must run the same DB version.

details.label: Used by
details.value: PostgreSQL streaming replication, Oracle Data Guard

items.reveal: Log describes changes at the row level: 'insert row with these column values', 'delete row with this primary key', 'update column X of row Y to value Z'.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Decoupled from storage engine. Leader and follower can run different versions or even different databases.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Easier to parse for external consumers (CDC, data pipelines)

details.label: Used by
details.value: MySQL binlog (row format), PostgreSQL logical decoding, Debezium CDC

items.reveal: Application-level triggers or stored procedures log changes to a separate table. An external process reads this table and replicates to other systems.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Most flexible — can filter, transform, or route changes with custom logic

details.label: Problem
details.value: Highest overhead. More bugs. Slower than built-in replication.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Oracle GoldenGate (historically), custom CDC solutions

action: Learn the main types of CRDTs and what each is used for.
description: Concept card: CRDT types — G-Counter, PN-Counter, G-Set, OR-Set, LWW-Register.
context: CRDTs solve a specific problem: how do you let multiple nodes update the same data independently, without any coordination, and guarantee they'll converge to the same result? Before CRDTs, conflict resolution required either a central coordinator (slow) or application-specific merge logic (error-prone). CRDTs are data structures mathematically designed so that merging is always safe, regardless of order or timing.
constraint_note: The key insight: CRDTs work because their merge operation is commutative, associative, and idempotent. Order doesn't matter, duplicates don't matter.
example: How a G-Counter merge works: Node A has counter [5, 3] (A incremented 5 times, last saw B at 3). Node B has counter [4, 7] (last saw A at 4, B incremented 7 times). To merge: take the MAX of each position → [max(5,4), max(3,7)] = [5, 7]. Total count = 5 + 7 = 12. This works regardless of merge order — Node A merging with B gives the same result as B merging with A. That's what 'commutative' means in practice.

items.reveal: Each node maintains its own counter. To increment: increase your own entry. To read: sum all entries. To merge: take max of each entry.

details.label: Example
details.value: Page view counter. Node A has [A:5, B:3], Node B has [A:4, B:7]. Merge: [A:5, B:7] = 12 total.

details.label: Limitation
details.value: Can only go up. No decrements.

items.reveal: Two G-Counters: one for increments (P), one for decrements (N). Value = sum(P) - sum(N). Supports both add and subtract.

details.label: Example
details.value: Like/unlike count. Upvote/downvote score.

details.label: How
details.value: Increment adds to P-counter, decrement adds to N-counter. Both are G-Counters internally.

items.reveal: Elements can be added but never removed. Merge is set union. Trivially convergent.

details.label: Example
details.value: Set of users who have ever visited a page. Set of tags ever applied.

details.label: Limitation
details.value: No removal. Once added, an element is permanent.

items.reveal: Supports both add and remove. Each add is tagged with a unique ID. Remove only removes the specific tagged versions the remover has seen, not future adds.

details.label: How
details.value: Add: insert (element, unique-tag). Remove: remove all tags for element that you've observed.

details.label: Advantage
details.value: Add wins over concurrent remove — if one node adds and another removes concurrently, the element stays.

details.label: Used by
details.value: Shopping carts, collaborative editing, shared lists

items.reveal: A single value with a timestamp. On merge, the value with the higher timestamp wins. Simple but can lose concurrent writes.

details.label: Trade-off
details.value: Same as LWW conflict resolution — simple convergence but silent data loss

details.label: Used by
details.value: Cassandra columns (each column is effectively an LWW-Register)

action: Fill in the blanks to test your understanding of quorum math.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: quorum formulas and configurations.
context: Quorum math is the foundation of leaderless replication. You need to be able to calculate whether a configuration provides consistency, and what happens when nodes fail.
constraint_note: Remember: R + W > N is the condition for guaranteed consistency. Think about what each variable means.

items.prompt: In quorum replication, the condition for guaranteed consistency is R + W > ___, where N is the number of ___.
items.hint: Read quorum + Write quorum must exceed total replicas

items.prompt: With N=5, W=3, R=3: R+W=___, which is ___ than N, so reads are ___ to see the latest write.
items.hint: 6 > 5, so read and write sets must overlap

items.prompt: With N=5, W=3, R=2: R+W=___, which ___ N, so stale reads are ___.
items.hint: 5 is NOT greater than 5 — no guaranteed overlap

items.prompt: A sloppy quorum allows writes to succeed by writing to ___ reachable nodes, even if they aren't the ___ designated replicas.
items.hint: Hinted handoff stores writes temporarily on non-home nodes

action: Fill in the blanks about leader-based replication and failover.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: leader replication, failover, and catch-up.
context: Leader failover is one of the most dangerous operations in a replicated database. Getting it wrong can cause data loss, split-brain, or extended downtime.
constraint_note: Think about what can go wrong at each step of failover.

items.prompt: In single-leader replication, all ___ go to the leader, while ___ can be served by any replica.
items.hint: One writer, many readers

items.prompt: When a leader fails with asynchronous replication, the new leader may be ___ the old leader, causing ___ of recent writes.
items.hint: Async means followers haven't received all writes yet

items.prompt: Split-brain occurs when ___ nodes both believe they are the leader and accept ___ independently.
items.hint: The most dangerous failure mode in leader-based replication

items.prompt: To prevent split-brain, systems use ___ tokens — a monotonically increasing number that lets other nodes reject requests from an old leader.
items.hint: The new leader gets a higher number; the old leader's requests are rejected

action: Fill in the blanks about conflict resolution and CRDTs.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: conflict resolution strategies and CRDT properties.
context: Conflict resolution is the hardest part of multi-leader and leaderless replication. Understanding the precise properties of each strategy prevents subtle data loss bugs.
constraint_note: Pay attention to what each strategy guarantees and what it silently loses.

items.prompt: Last-write-wins (LWW) is simple but can silently ___ concurrent writes. Only the write with the highest ___ survives.
items.hint: All other concurrent writes vanish without notification

items.prompt: CRDTs guarantee convergence because their merge operation is ___, ___, and idempotent.
items.hint: Order doesn't matter (commutative), grouping doesn't matter (associative)

items.prompt: A G-Counter works by having each node maintain its own counter. The total value is the ___ of all node counters. Merge takes the ___ of each entry.
items.hint: Node A:[5,3] + Node B:[4,7] → merge:[5,7] → total: 12

items.prompt: In an OR-Set, when add and remove happen concurrently, ___ wins — the element remains in the set.
items.hint: Observed-Remove: remove only removes versions you've seen, not future adds

action: Fill in the blanks about leaderless replication mechanisms.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: read repair, anti-entropy, hinted handoff.
context: Leaderless systems need background mechanisms to keep replicas in sync. These three mechanisms handle different scenarios — understanding when each kicks in is essential.
constraint_note: Read repair is reactive (triggered by reads). Anti-entropy is proactive (background process). Hinted handoff handles temporary failures.

items.prompt: Read repair works by detecting stale data during a ___ and sending the latest value to the ___ replica.
items.hint: The read itself fixes the inconsistency it discovers

items.prompt: Anti-entropy is a ___ process that compares replicas and copies missing data. It often uses ___ trees to efficiently find differences.
items.hint: Runs continuously, not triggered by reads

items.prompt: Hinted handoff: when a node is down, another node temporarily ___ writes meant for it. When the node recovers, the hints are ___.
items.hint: A temporary stand-in that remembers what the absent node missed

items.prompt: A sloppy quorum with hinted handoff improves write ___ but does NOT guarantee read consistency, because hints may be on nodes outside the ___ quorum.
items.hint: The W nodes that acknowledged may not be the R nodes you read from

action: Fill in the blanks about multi-leader replication.
description: Fill-in-the-blank: multi-leader replication use cases and challenges.
context: Multi-leader replication is used less often than single-leader, but it's essential for multi-datacenter deployments and collaborative editing. The key challenge is always conflict handling.
constraint_note: Multi-leader is powerful but complex. Most teams avoid it unless they specifically need multi-region writes.

items.prompt: The main use case for multi-leader replication is ___ operation, where each datacenter has its own leader for low-latency local writes.
items.hint: Users in each region write to a local leader

items.prompt: The biggest challenge with multi-leader replication is handling write ___ — when two leaders accept conflicting writes to the same data.
items.hint: Two leaders, two different values for the same key

items.prompt: Conflict ___ avoids conflicts entirely by routing all writes for a particular record to the ___ leader.
items.hint: If all writes for user X go to datacenter A, there's no conflict for user X

items.prompt: Multi-leader replication topologies include circular, star, and ___. The ___ topology is most fault-tolerant because every node sends to every other node.
items.hint: In circular and star, one node failure can break the replication chain

action: Compare single-leader vs multi-leader replication. Understand when each is appropriate.
description: Trade-off comparison: single-leader vs multi-leader replication.
context: This is one of the first architectural decisions when designing a replicated database. Most systems use single-leader. Multi-leader adds complexity but enables multi-region writes.
constraint_note: Single-leader is the default choice. Only use multi-leader when you specifically need writes in multiple datacenters with low latency.
example: A global e-commerce site might use multi-leader so users in Europe can write to a European leader without cross-Atlantic latency.

pairs.left: Single-Leader — one node accepts all writes. No write conflicts possible. Simple to reason about. Leader is a write bottleneck and single point of failure. Failover can lose data. Used by: PostgreSQL, MySQL, MongoDB.
pairs.right: Multi-Leader — multiple nodes accept writes independently. Enables low-latency writes in multiple regions. Write conflicts are inevitable and must be resolved. Much more complex. Used by: CouchDB, Tungsten, collaborative editing apps.

action: Compare read repair vs anti-entropy as mechanisms for keeping leaderless replicas in sync.
description: Trade-off comparison: read repair vs anti-entropy for replica convergence.
context: In leaderless replication, replicas can diverge. These two mechanisms bring them back into sync, but they work differently and have different trade-offs.
constraint_note: Read repair only fixes data that's actually read. Anti-entropy fixes everything but uses more resources. Most systems use both.
example: If a key is written but never read again, read repair will never fix a stale replica for that key. Anti-entropy will.

pairs.left: Read Repair — triggered during reads. When a quorum read detects a stale replica, it sends the latest value to that replica. Reactive: only fixes data that's actually read. Low overhead but leaves unread data inconsistent. Fast for hot data.
pairs.right: Anti-Entropy — background process that continuously compares replicas and copies missing data. Uses Merkle trees to efficiently find differences. Proactive: fixes all data, even if never read. Higher resource usage but ensures full convergence.

action: Compare strict quorum vs sloppy quorum. Understand the availability-consistency trade-off.
description: Trade-off comparison: strict quorum vs sloppy quorum with hinted handoff.
context: When some of the N designated nodes are unreachable, you have a choice: refuse the operation (strict) or use substitute nodes (sloppy). This is a direct availability vs consistency trade-off.
constraint_note: Sloppy quorums improve availability but break the R + W > N consistency guarantee because hints may be on non-standard nodes.
example: DynamoDB uses sloppy quorums by default — it prioritizes availability. If you need strong consistency, you must explicitly request it.

pairs.left: Strict Quorum — writes and reads only count responses from the N designated home nodes. If fewer than W home nodes are reachable, the write fails. Guarantees R + W > N consistency. Lower availability during node failures.
pairs.right: Sloppy Quorum + Hinted Handoff — writes can go to any reachable node, even non-home nodes. Hints are forwarded to home nodes when they recover. Higher write availability. But reads may not see recent writes because hints are on non-home nodes.

action: Read the scenario and analyze what happens during leader failover with async replication.
description: What-breaks scenario: leader failover causes data loss.
context: Leader failover with asynchronous replication is one of the most dangerous operations in database management. This scenario tests whether you understand exactly what can go wrong.
constraint_note: Think step by step: what was replicated, what wasn't, and what happens to the unreplicated writes.
example: This is not a theoretical problem — it happens in production. GitHub had a famous incident in 2018 caused by exactly this scenario with MySQL.

reference.body:
You run PostgreSQL with one primary and two replicas using asynchronous streaming replication. The replicas are typically 500ms behind the primary.

At 14:00:00.000, the primary accepts a write: INSERT INTO orders (id, amount) VALUES (9999, $500).
At 14:00:00.200, the primary crashes (hardware failure). The write has NOT yet been replicated to either replica.

Your automated failover promotes Replica A to be the new primary.

Analyze:
1. What happened to order 9999? Can it be recovered?
2. If the old primary's disk is intact and it comes back online, what should happen to it?
3. What if the old primary comes back and starts accepting writes before anyone notices it's no longer the leader?
4. How would synchronous replication change this scenario?
5. What's the trade-off of switching to synchronous replication?

action: Read the scenario and analyze the split-brain problem.
description: What-breaks scenario: split-brain in a two-node cluster.
context: Split-brain is the most dangerous failure mode in leader-based replication. Two nodes both accepting writes leads to divergent data that may be impossible to reconcile.
constraint_note: Think about: how did this happen, what's the damage, and how do you prevent it in the future?
example: This is why most systems require an odd number of nodes and use majority-based leader election.

reference.body:
You have a two-node PostgreSQL cluster: Node A (primary) and Node B (replica). They use a heartbeat to detect failures.

A network partition occurs — A and B can't communicate, but both are running fine and reachable by different sets of clients.

Node B doesn't receive heartbeats from A, concludes A is dead, and promotes itself to primary. Now both A and B are accepting writes.

Clients in the US write to Node A. Clients in EU write to Node B. Both nodes accept writes to the same tables for 10 minutes before the partition heals.

Analyze:
1. What's the state of the data when the partition heals? Can you merge the two divergent histories?
2. Why did a two-node cluster make this problem worse?
3. How does a three-node cluster with majority-based election prevent this?
4. What is a fencing token and how would it help here?
5. What should the system do when the partition heals and it discovers two primaries?

action: Read the scenario and identify which replication lag problem is occurring and how to fix it.
description: What-breaks scenario: user sees stale data after updating their profile.
context: Replication lag bugs are the most common distributed systems issues in web applications. They're hard to reproduce because they depend on which replica serves the read.
constraint_note: Identify the specific replication lag problem by name. Then propose a fix that doesn't require switching to synchronous replication.
example: The three replication lag problems are: read-your-writes, monotonic reads, and consistent prefix reads.

reference.body:
Your web application uses PostgreSQL with one primary and three read replicas. A load balancer distributes read traffic across replicas.

Scenario 1: User updates their display name from 'John' to 'Jonathan'. The write goes to the primary. The user's browser immediately refreshes the profile page. The load balancer sends this read to Replica C, which is 2 seconds behind. The user sees 'John' and thinks the update failed. They submit the form again.

Scenario 2: User Alice reads a comment thread. She sees 5 comments. She refreshes. The load balancer sends this read to a different replica that's further behind. She now sees only 3 comments. She refreshes again and sees 5 again.

Scenario 3: In a chat, User B posts 'What time is the meeting?' User C replies 'It's at 3pm.' A third user sees 'It's at 3pm' appear BEFORE 'What time is the meeting?' because the reply was on a partition that replicated faster.

For each scenario:
1. Name the specific replication lag problem.
2. Explain why it happened.
3. Propose a fix.

action: Read the scenario and analyze what happens to quorum operations during a network partition.
description: What-breaks scenario: network partition splits a 5-node quorum cluster.
context: Quorum systems handle partitions differently depending on which side of the partition you're on. This scenario tests whether you can reason through the math.
constraint_note: Calculate whether each side can achieve quorum for reads and writes. Think about what happens to clients on each side.
example: With N=5, W=3, R=3: the side with 3 nodes can still operate. The side with 2 nodes cannot.

reference.body:
You run a Cassandra cluster with 5 nodes (N=5), configured with W=3 and R=3 (strict quorum). A network partition splits the cluster into two groups: Group A has 3 nodes, Group B has 2 nodes.

Analyze:
1. Can Group A accept writes? Can it serve consistent reads? Why?
2. Can Group B accept writes? Can it serve consistent reads? Why?
3. A client on Group B's side tries to write. What happens?
4. What if you changed the configuration to W=2, R=2? How would the partition behavior change?
5. What if you used sloppy quorum instead of strict quorum? What changes?

action: Walk through the write path in single-leader replication, step by step.
description: Protocol walkthrough: single-leader replication write path — from client to all replicas.
context: Understanding the exact sequence of events during a write helps you reason about where failures can occur and what data might be lost.
constraint_note: Pay attention to the difference between synchronous and asynchronous steps. The sync/async boundary is where data loss risk lives.
example: This is how PostgreSQL streaming replication, MySQL replication, and MongoDB replica sets all work at a high level.

steps: Client sends a write request (e.g., UPDATE users SET name='Alice' WHERE id=1)
Request arrives at the leader node. Leader writes to its local storage and write-ahead log (WAL).
Leader confirms the write to the client. (In async replication, this happens NOW — before replicas are updated.)
Leader sends the WAL entry (or logical change record) to all follower replicas.
Each follower receives the change and applies it to its local storage in the same order.
In SYNCHRONOUS replication: step 3 happens AFTER at least one follower confirms. This guarantees the write survives leader failure but adds latency.
In ASYNCHRONOUS replication: step 3 happens BEFORE followers confirm. Lower latency but the write can be lost if the leader crashes before step 4 completes.
If a follower is temporarily down, it catches up by requesting all WAL entries it missed since its last known position.
Key insight: the window between step 3 (client confirmation) and step 5 (follower apply) is the 'replication lag' — and it's where all the consistency problems live.

action: Walk through how chain replication works — a different approach to replicating data.
description: Protocol walkthrough: chain replication — writes flow head to tail, reads served from tail.
context: Chain replication (from the 2004 OSDI paper) is an alternative to leader-based replication used in systems like Microsoft Azure Storage and HDFS. It separates the read and write paths for better throughput.
constraint_note: The key insight: the tail has ALL committed writes, so reads from the tail are always consistent. No quorum needed for reads.
example: Covered in MIT 6.824 Lecture 10. Used in production by Microsoft Azure Storage.

steps: Nodes are arranged in a chain: Head → Node B → Node C → Tail
WRITE PATH: Client sends write to the Head node.
Head applies the write locally, then forwards it to Node B.
Node B applies locally, forwards to Node C. Node C applies, forwards to Tail.
Tail applies the write and sends acknowledgment back to the client. The write is now committed.
READ PATH: Client sends read to the Tail node. Tail has all committed writes, so reads are always consistent.
NODE FAILURE (middle): If Node B fails, Head is reconfigured to send directly to Node C. The chain shortens.
NODE FAILURE (head): The next node becomes the new head. Some in-flight writes may be lost if not yet forwarded.
NODE FAILURE (tail): The previous node becomes the new tail. It may have writes the old tail hadn't acknowledged yet — these are now committed.

Key trade-off: Write latency is proportional to chain length (must traverse all nodes). But read throughput is high because reads only hit the tail, and the tail is never busy with write coordination.

action: Listen to (or read) the story of a split-brain incident, then answer the comprehension questions.
description: Narration: a split-brain incident in a database cluster — what went wrong and how it was resolved.
context: Split-brain is one of the most feared failure modes in distributed systems. This story illustrates how it happens in practice and why it's so hard to recover from.
constraint_note: Pay attention to the sequence of events and the decisions made at each step.
example: After this, you should understand why split-brain prevention is a critical design requirement.

segments.text: It was 2 AM when the pager went off. The database cluster had a problem.
The team ran a two-node MySQL cluster with automatic failover. Node A was the primary, Node B the replica.
A network switch failed, cutting communication between the two nodes. But both nodes were still running and reachable by different application servers.
The failover system on Node B detected that it couldn't reach Node A. It concluded Node A was dead and promoted Node B to primary.
Now both nodes were accepting writes. Application servers in rack 1 wrote to Node A. Servers in rack 2 wrote to Node B. For 8 minutes, the database diverged.
When the network switch was replaced, the team discovered the split-brain. 847 orders had been written to Node A, 612 to Node B. Some orders existed on both nodes with different data.
Recovery took 6 hours. They had to manually compare every conflicting row, contact customers, and reconcile the data. Some data was permanently lost.
The root cause: a two-node cluster cannot safely do automatic failover. With only two nodes, neither can achieve a majority to confirm the other is truly dead.

questions.prompt: Why did the split-brain happen?
questions.answer: Network partition between two nodes. Node B falsely concluded Node A was dead and promoted itself. Both accepted writes.

questions.prompt: Why is a two-node cluster especially vulnerable to split-brain?
questions.answer: Neither node can achieve a majority (need 2 of 2). There's no way to distinguish 'other node is dead' from 'network is partitioned'.

questions.prompt: How would a three-node cluster prevent this?
questions.answer: With 3 nodes, a majority is 2. The side with 2 nodes can elect a leader; the isolated single node cannot. Only one side accepts writes.

questions.prompt: What is a fencing token and how would it help?
